content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 19, 1995

Honorable Jim Morrissey
State Capitol, Room 448
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-94-400

Dear Assemblyman Morrissey:

You have requested advice concerning your reporting
obligations under the conflict of interest disclosure provisions
of the Political Reform Act "the Act".1/ On January 12, 1995,
Dave Stefanides of your staff confirmed that the request for
advice concerns your reporting obligations. Since your letter
states a general question, we are treating your letter as a
request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).2/

QUESTION

Are you required to report free "stand by" passes you receive
as a result of your son's employment agreement as an airline pilot
on your statement of economic interests (Form 721)?

CONCLUSION

You are not required to report "stand by" passes you receive
that are provided by your son's employer if they are received as a
result of his employment agreement and there was no intent by the
airline to personally give you gifts of free passes based on your
status as an officeholder.

_______________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Assemblyman Morrissey
Our File No. I-94-400
Page Two

FACTS

Your son is an airline pilot. As part of his employment
agreement, you and your spouse are provided free "stand by" passes
for his airline.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act requires elected state officers to
disclose reportable investments, interests in real property and
sources of income on a statement of economic interests (Form 71).
(Section 87200, et seq.)

Section 82030 defines "income", in part, as:

[A]ny payment received, including but not limited to any
salary, wage, advance....

Section 82044 defines "payment" as:

A payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance,
deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services
or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible.

The Commission has previously advised that where an
individual provides full and adequate consideration to his or her
employer, usual and customary payments provided by an employer as
part of earned compensation are treated as income. (Section
82028; Section 82030; Khan Advice Letter, No. A-91-490.)
Therefore, it would appear that the free "stand by" passes are
provided by your son's employer as compensation for services
rendered.

Section 82028 defines "gift" as:

[A]ny payment to the extent that consideration of equal
or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate
or discount is made in the regular course of business to
members of the public without regard to official status.

The Commission has also advised that if there are no
circumstances present to indicate that the airline intended to
personally give you gifts of free "stand by" passes, and the use
of the passes is controlled by your son as part of his employment
agreement, they are considered a gift to you from your son.
(Combs Advice Letter, No. A-87-141, copy enclosed.) A gift from
one's son is not a "gift" within the meaning of the Act and is not
reportable on a statement of economic interests. (Section
82028(b)(3).)

Assemblyman Morrissey
Our File No. I-94-400
Page Three


If you have any questions or need further assistance, please
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Dixie Howard
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

Enclosure
